title: The Twenty-third Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
November 14, 2022 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

## LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on the Twenty-third Sunday after Pentecost in the liturgical calendar, placing the congregation in Ordinary Time following the long season after Pentecost. The Revised Common Lectionary for this date would have included readings from 1 Thessalonians 5:1-11 (on watchfulness), Matthew 25:14-30 (the parable of the talents), and Zephaniah 1:7, 12-18 as the Old Testament reading. This Sunday fell in the final weeks of Ordinary Time before the First Sunday of Advent (November 27, 2022), making this a reflective moment on stewardship and preparedness before the church year's renewal.

## EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period of significant internal debate regarding sexual ethics and LGBTQ+ inclusion, with these tensions having been heightened at the 80th General Convention in Austin (July 2022) where resolutions affirmed LGBTQ+ inclusion but some conservative dioceses remained in conflict. Broderick Greer, known as a progressive voice within the denomination, was serving during a time when the Colorado Diocese under Bishop Kimberly Lucas (who began her tenure in 2021) was navigating these national divisions while maintaining pastoral presence in the Rocky Mountain region. No major diocesan conventions or bishop elections were scheduled for November 2022, but the denomination's ongoing realignment around theological differences was the backdrop for all preaching.

## DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing early November weather transitions typical for the season, with the region preparing for winter conditions ahead. Colorado politics were focused on the immediate aftermath of the November 8, 2022 midterm elections (held just six days prior), where Colorado voters had rejected several conservative ballot measures and reelected Democratic Governor Jared Polis with a substantial margin, while also electing Democratic Jena Griswold as Secretary of State. The Capitol Hill neighborhood surrounding Saint John's Cathedral was experiencing typical post-election civic engagement and conversation about the state's future political direction.

## NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The midterm elections from November 8 were the dominant national story, with Democrats performing better than historical precedent for a sitting president's party, gaining Senate seats and limiting House Republican gains to a smaller margin than anticipated. Elon Musk's recent acquisition of Twitter (finalized October 27) continued to dominate tech and cultural news with mass layoffs and chaotic policy changes unfolding throughout early November. The Robert Babbitt coal plant closure in New Mexico (announced in this period) represented the ongoing national energy transition and climate policy debates affecting the Rocky Mountain West specifically.

## INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Russia's continued invasion of Ukraine (ongoing since February 2022) remained a major international story, with Ukrainian forces having recently liberated Kherson on November 11, marking a significant military and symbolic shift in the war after Russia's withdrawal across the Dnipro River. The COP27 climate conference was convening in Sharm El-Sheikh, Egypt (November 6-20), focusing global attention on climate commitments and climate finance for developing nations. North Korea conducted a ballistic missile test on November 3, continuing its weapons development program amid regional tensions on the Korean peninsula.

## CULTURAL MOMENT

The national mood in mid-November 2022 was characterized by cautious optimism among Democrats following stronger-than-expected midterm results, while many Americans remained anxious about inflation, which had peaked at 9.1% in June but remained stubbornly high at 7.7% in October. The cultural zeitgeist was one of deep political polarization, social media-fueled division (exacerbated by Twitter's destabilization), and generalized uncertainty about economic stability. Denver's specifically was one of progressive confidence following election results, with a notable cultural focus on the city's ongoing growth, Colorado's climate leadership positioning, and the region's identity as a destination for people seeking both outdoor recreation and urban amenities.
